CURRENT AFFAIRS,

There is to be a Feis and Industrial Exhibition at
Tullamore next week. It badly wants both. Tulla-

- more, to the passing visitor’s eye, does not appear to be

a very prosperous place, and both its intellect and its
industry would be the better of a good shaking. The

lanes and cabins appear to be, even for an Irish town,
somewhat out of proportion to the whole.  The first
things you see on emerging from the railway station are

- - the Jail and the Court House, fit outposts for an Irish
2 town, as Irish towns are to-day, after upwards - f a
century of British rule. There are many signs in Tulla-
more of the Industrial Revival in so far as that Revival
has been effected by operating on the consumer.. To
some considerable extent Irish goods are displaged in
‘the shop windows, and no doubt the work of the local
Industrial Committee will develop matters further in
 that. direction. The Tullamore Industrial Committee
cently published a useful directory of goods made in
nd with special regard to goods locally made. Yet

‘veached a position of assured success.”’

z

still in the square the visitor observes foreign agricul-
tural machinery glaring the public in the face like
British impudence. Some Irish agricultural machinery
was to be seen also; but would it be too much to sug-
gest that the onus of asking for British goods ought to
be loft on the purchaser. There is still room for an
expanded sale of Murphy’s starch, Irish jam, boots,
boot polish, Irish cigarettes, Irvish soap and matches in
Tullamore; and we hope the forthcoming Industrial
Exhibition will have its effect on the sale of these com-
modities.

Certaiuly Tullamore suffers from no lack of public
houses; and wherein the weary traveller is stowed at
some of the houses that announce “lodgings’’ over the
doors is a question to excite a passing wonder.  The
word “ Entertainment ’’ figures also on the sign-boards
of some establishments, and ’s Entertainment ’
did not look very inviting. We noticed that the “Irish
Evangelization Society ’’ were about to operate in the
town. A bill with the usual “ All are welcome’’ ar-
rested the attention of the passer-by who looked through
the window of a shop with the name Duggan over the
door. Some British boot polish rested calmly in the
window also. A solitary bobby stood languidly in the
middle of the road, lonesome looking, without a con-
certina or a fishing rod, but his boots were dimly shining
—probably the polish was imported—so that no doubt
he could look back on half of one of the days of his
useful and energetic life, and then, gazing at his brogues
as he stood earning his pay in the middle of the road,
exclaim “ Something attempted, something done.”’

There was a “mission ”” being held in the Catholic
church—a rough temporary wooden structure which,
however, because of its roughness and rawness, made
a strong appeal to one. A light, as it were, from the
Penal Days played about the uncouth interior that

gtimulated  devotion—stimulated also  that na-
tional passion and vracial pride “which _ help
so strongly to combine together the Irish
nation and  the TIrish church. The tem-

porary building is doing duty pending the completion of
what promises to be a fine church. The familiar stalls
associated with  Missions’’ were clustered round the
place, and the usual gaudy prints were on sale in
abundance. How i it that some of the excellent pic-
tures, reproductions of originals worth reproducing, that
were issued as supplements by the Zrish Rosary are not
sold at all these “ Mission ” stalls? g

We have received a letter from a British Dumping
Clothing Firm.  The letter is as follows:—“T have
pleasure in sonding you herewith a report of the open-
ing of our new Rifle Range, by Field-Marshal Earl
Roberts, which doubtless will be of much interest to
your readers. I hope that you will invite firms in your
locality who are in a position to do so, to provide similar
facilities for their employees.” This Dumping British
firm must have dug our name promiscuous like out of a
Press directory, or else they would not commit them-
selves to the opinion that this advertising scheme of
theirs “will be of much interest to your readers.” As far
as inviting firms in our locality go we would invite
them to erect rifle ranges in’ which all sorts of foreign
manufactured stuff that can be kept out of the country
on sound business and economic principles should be
shot at by the sensible and patriotic shopkeepers of this
country.

“ Certainly the veto of Mr. John Redmond and the
sneers of the LEADER constitute no valid cause for
wavering in the promotion of a scheme which has now
So wrote ¢l

- man, Mike McCarthy, in a letter which appeared in
~ Lord Bung’s Ezpress in February, 1904, favouring the
obnoxious scheme of an International Exhibition. This
creature, McCarthy, was then trying to foist himself
d on the electors of the Stephen’s Green Division, and he
stated in his letter to Ardilaun’s Fapress:— As it is
in my capacity of candidate for the Stephen’s Green
Division that the queries on the point have been ad-
dressed to me, let me say that, T shall support the exhi-
_ bition in the interests of the trade and commerce of
- the city by every means in my power, and if elected
shall exert myself to secure the largest Government
A Grant possible for so patriotic an undertaking.”” Where
is Mike “ the Catholic”’ now? He got the nasty kick
out from Stephen’s Green, and has since, we believe,
turned his attention to the Japs. Show me your com-
pany and I’ll tell you what you are. The fact that
Mike was a supporter of what has turned out to be
Humphrey’s Dump, is enough to give any decent Irish-
man pause.

The arrangements for the forthcoming Industrial Con-
ference at Cork are evidently in a forward state. We
have already received a programme of the Conference
which will be opened on Tuesday, November 21st. The
opening ceremony of the Exhibition will commence at
3.30 on the previous Monday.

Mullingar does not appear to err on the temperance
side, neither does Athlone. In the course of Judge
Curran’s address to the Grand Jury at Mullingar Quar-
ter Sessions, he said:—“1I see by the morning papers
that they are starting in Dublin a crusade against in-
temperance, and I wish sincerely that they would send

»_ an apostle down to Athlone and let him call on the way
to Mullingar.” In the course of one of the cases, a
case from Athlone, one Hugh Broghan, stated that a
Mrs. Mee was sending out for drink wholesale and that
he remained until he got drunk. His Honour: “More
shame for you.”” Broughan: “I don’t see where the
shame comes in. Any man will take drink when he
can get it for nothing.”” Evidently the Anti-Treating
League has a wide scope for its work in Athlone.

The public obstruction in the centre of Dublin, called
Nelson’s Pillar, was dressed up on Saturday last; a flag
also obtruded itself out of one of the windows of the
Erasmus Smith school in Harcourt Street. There was
a special sermon at the cathedral of the Church of Eng-
land’s Faithful Garrison, and a song was sung at the
Empire, late Dan Lowry’s Music Hall, on Saturday
night. Nelson is responsible for the saying that “ Eng-
land expects every man to do his duty.”” No doubt the
shareholders of the Music Hall in Crampton Court ex-
pected the man Figgis to do his duty; he did more
than his duty, however, for he decamped with some
eight hundred pounds of the shareholders’ money. It
is poor consolation to the shareholders that Nelson was
honoured in song at the music hall. Now if someone
would catch Figgis for them and make him do his duty
by refunding that money, there would be occasion for
rejoicing. .

an appreciative and lengthy article on “ M. O’R’s. re-

Mr, Matthew J. Byrne is Horticulture Iu§bru%;701; in
the County of Waterford. A local paper, Lh'c a er;
ford Star, refers to Mr. Byrne as «the horticulturis
who has been doing such eminently usg[ul and neces-
sary work since his appointment to this county, .nog
some six months ago.’”” Mr. Byrne was fully qualifie
for the post else he would not have got it. He is a
graduate of Athy.and Dublin Model Farms; he has h:fd
three years’ practical experience, with a nurseryman in
California, U.S.A., two years’ experience In Canada,
and two years in Australia. Theoretically and practi-
cally Mr. Byrne ought to be competent if any man is.
Yot the order has gone forth form the Dublin Castle
Department, over which Walter Long, the Orange-
mon’s Chief Secrotary presides, that Mr. Matthew J.
Byrne is to be superseded. We have not heard who it
is proposed to shovel into his place, or whether or not
the new man comes from Scotland. It is a curious, 3?
not a significant, fact that Mr. Byrne is an ‘_‘ Idolator.
We hope that the Waterford County Council and other

local bodies interested in the spread of horticultural -

knowledge will resolutely demand the reason why of
Mr. Byrne’s proposed dismissal by the Castle Depart-
ment over which Walter Long presides.

A correspondent sends us a discouraging account of
Omagh in relation to the Irish Revival. At the same
time he hopefully remarks:—* However, with the good
example of Dromore (where, by the way, they can stage
Irish plays to crowded audiences), the persevering
energy of a few, the zealous endeavours of the new 12095
Rev. Father O’Doherty, together with the march of
events, things must, in the near future, take a turn for
the better.”” We hope so.

What do the British Liberal Party intend to do with
Ireland when—or if—they get in? That is an interest-
ing question to us and to our readers; but it is the
special business of those who take the responsibility of
running the political department of Irish Endeavour
on the floor of the British House of Commons. What
are the Party doing by way of bringing the British
Liberals to toe the Irish line? Foreigners like Camp-
bell-Bannerman, Asquith and others whose names we
notice in the papers have been making speeches; but
we have so many Irish matters to look after that we
have no time to follow the sayings of these foreigners
any more than we have the speeches of French or Ger-
man politicians. But if the Liberal Party vefuse to
put Home Rule for Ireland on their programme, what
is L‘,; be gained by helping to turn the other scoundrels
out?

Does an ‘“ Idolatrous *’ stationmaster ever get a testi-

monial? We see that one Mr. Tombe is being removed
from Castlebar of the “Races’ on promotion. Mz,
Tombe is 7ot an “Tdolator.”” A preliminary meeting
was held at the “ Imperial > Hotel in the town of the
red coat races, for the purposes of inflicting a testi-
monial to Mr, Tombe on a series of natives in un-
happy Castlebar. What has this man Toombe ever
done that a testimonial to him should be inflicted on
poverty-stricken Castlebar? Are there no orphans in
Castlebar that have a prior claim on the gencrosity of
those with money to spare in that town than has
Tombe the promoted stationmaster. We believe that
one Dr. Brabazon, a Mr. Morrison, a Mr. Reid, and
a Mr. Robertson who with others are conspiring to in-
flict this testimonial on unhappy Castlebar, ave, like
Mr. Tombe, “Saved.”

We strongly advise the people of Castlebar to keep
their money in their pockets if they have no better use
for it than to give it to the promoted Mr. Tombe. If
the likely subscribers to this testimonial had all been
promoted to better jobs than they had, and if Mr.
Tombe had been reduced, there might be some sense in
asking each to give a trifle to Mr. Tombe; but as Mr.

CORRESPONDENCE.
Irisa Music.

Dear Sie—Allow me to supplement on one point the
graceful and pleasant sketch of my lecture con-
tributed to your columns last week by “ Lee.”” I want
to make my views as to.the harmonisation of Irish airs
a little more clear, as this subject has of late heen some-
what warmly discussed.

Our airs naturally fall into three classes:—(1) Those
in modern scales, (2) those in old modal scales, and (3)
those in ““ gapped ”’ scales. Airs of the first class may,
of course, be arranged for voices or instruments accord-
ing to ordinary rules; though I think good taste would
_suggest a very restrained use of chromatic and dissonant
harmony. In this class would be included nearly all
the airs in Moore—going hastily down the index I
votice only three exceptions—and many airs in Petrie,
Joyce, etc. Airs of this class are also amenable to the
_exigencies of part-writing as regards time.

Airs of the second class—viz., those in old modal
scales, must be viewed differently. They belong to a

_period before the evolution of modern harmony. Their
scales have long ago dropped out of ‘general use, and
50 their harmonic treatment is not provided for in our
present system. These airs, being the oldest, are the
most precious part of our musical inheritance, and
should be zealously guarded from ignorant meddling.
Few modern musicians have any idea of how they
should be harmonised. Witness the unsatisfactory at-
tempts hitherto made to write accompaniments for the
older Gregorian chants. A scheme of harmonisation,
or, at Jeast, hints towards such a scheme, might be de-
duced from a study of the compositions of the 16th and
16th centuries.  But this is a work which, so far as I
know, has.yet to be done. I would venture to say of
these old airs:—(1) They are probably not suited for
part-singing, resembling in this the Gregorian Chant,
o which they are closely allied. Part-treatment would
almost certainly interfere also with the extreme free-
 dom with which they should be sung; (2) nor do I

THE LEADER.

OcToBER 28, 1905.

think them suited for even a confinuous accompani-
ment. A broken accompaniment of sympathetic
chords, arpeggios, imitation or “echo’’ phrases, etc.,
such as they probably got from the old harpers, would
soom to be most suifable; (3) the chords and progres-
sions should be of the old-fashioned kind, modern chro-
matic and dissonant harmony being quite out of place.
Airs of the third class—viz., those in “gapped
seales, offer still greater difficulties. What about the
absent notes?  Should they appear in the harmony?
Can they be introduced without obscuring the charac-
toristic foature of the air? Yet without them how are
we to get chords in the modorn sense of the term?
Here is virgin soil for musical enterprise. Wanted—
a system of harmony for old Irish airs in gapped scales.
—Yours, etc.,
St. Vincent’s Church,
Cork.

E. GAYNOR.

A Caps,—The Gacelic League of London intends to
hold its third Aonach at the beginning of November,
and hopes to make it a great success.

The two former Aonachs were, to a considerable ex-
tent, successful, but it was very disappointing for us in
London to find on sale so few of the cheap, useful
anticles that are constantly advertised in THE LEADER
and other Irish-Ireland papers.

Some people in Ireland appear to suffer from the de-
lusion that we in London have money galore, and that
we would look with disdain on cheap, useful goods as
mere rubbish. Well, T am sorry to undeceive these
good people, but, as a matter of fact, we have no money
to spare, and what we want most are cheap, useful
articles. TLast year a brush manufacturer sent over a
large number of brushes useful for the stable and
coach house. I believe he sold one, and no doubt he
declares that we are not sincere in our support of the
industrial vevival. Well, if he had sent over brushes
that could be used in common households where horses
and carriages are unknown, he would, I am sure, have
formed a different idea of us.

What we want most are such everyday articles as
jams, sauces, oatmeal, etc. These we would not only
buy readily at the Aonach, but we would get our local
shopkeepers to stock them afterwards. Let Ireland send
us goods like these, and we will respond and do our
best to aid her in exporting goods in place of human
beings. P. Seasgan Ua Deotam.

ANTI-TREATING, BUNG, AND POLITICS.

S the present not an opportune time for a combined
]1 effort with a view of inducing the country to grasp
Mr. Bung? There has been a good deal of sparring-out
at Mr. Bung, but the country has not yet, as a whole,
engaged him at close quarters. We are sick of hearing,
we are tired ourselves of repeating, the ominous figure,
fourteen million pounds sterling per annum.

We hear that the Anti-Treating Campaign in the
diocese of Clonfert was a great success, and we are in-
formed that there was no drunkenness at the fair of
Ballinasloe. Now if Mr. Bung was tackled in real
earnest in the diocese of Clonfert, why not attack him
simultaneously in all the dioceses of Ireland? Probably
the result of the Clonfert campaign is being awaited
before the Hierarchy arrange a general attack of na-
tional dimensions. We believe, as a priest in a letter
to us recently put it, we believe the people are
thoroughly prepared for “a rising’’ against the drink
evil, and if they do not “rise’’ against Bung, the end
of it may be that Bung will drown the country, includ-
ing himself.

There is no use in being quixotic in the drink-evil
campaign ; ill-considered, quixotic campaigns are a sort
of drunkenness themselves, and a reaction inevitably
follows. Bung’s wings must be clipped on workmanlike,
business principles. Bung is a big item in this country,
and the man who collects £14,000,000 a year—a large
part of which goes to the British Treasury that is wish-
ful to rob the teachers of Irish of their due—cannot be
snuffed out in a month. There are very few people in
the country who have not Buung relatives, and then let
it be admitted in fairness that, personally, Bung is
often, if not usually, a genial fellow. It is as well to
admit also that in the present state of Irish human na-
ture and in the present state of the Irish climate, any-
thing like general teetotalism is out of the question. If
we could knock say a modest £6,000,000 off the drink
bill the country might congratulate itself, and it is hard
to see how retail Bung generally would be any the
worse off ; for most Bungs sell other things besides beer
and whiskey, and they no doubt feel easier in their
conscience in selling other things; and a man who saves
five pounds a year by joining the Anti-Treating League
is not going to throw his savings into the sea. In all
probability the publicans would materially gain if the
country docked its drink bill by £6,000,000 a year and
spent the money on food and other things. 3

A good ‘national combined effort in the interests
primarily of the Anti-Treating idea would do a world

of good just now, and the campaign could scoop in as
much teetotalism as it was able. If the Anti-Treating
League could be pushed simultancously all over the
country its chances of staying would be enormously in-
creased. At present a Clonfert Anti-Treater may be
fairly safe within the boundaries of the diocese, but if
he went outside, an environment would not be there to
give him what we might call collateral support; and
stronger and sterner men than many of those who habi-
tually take “ the pledge >’ for life can do with the sup-
port of a helpful environment. We do not believe in
abusing publicans, we never abuse them ourselves, but
neither do we spare them, and they should not be
spared, for they are partly drowning the nation. Tow-
-ever, Bung being often personally a very decent fellow,
many people spare his feelings to a degree which is noth-
ing but moral weakness.

Besides a general campaign from the pulpit, two other
means might be availed of.  Temperance advocates
might make more use of the Press than they do, and
reports of all sorts and kinds of temperance activities
should be sent to the local and other papers. It may
be said, that’s all very well, but who are to do these
things? We might retort, what is Mr. Moriarty’s All
Creeds, All Classes League doing? That League realised
a lot of money out of the All Ireland Bazaar, and it
were about time some stir was made with it.

Father Aloysius, of Church Street, complained at the
Catholic Truth Conference that temperance was not a
plank in the political national platform. Well, why
not alter, or attempt to alter, that? An opportunity
will be available at the forthcoming political conven-
tion. We believe that all priests are free to attend
fthese conventions, and we would suggest that the Church

| Street men and others go there and unfurl the tem-
| perance standard in the name of God and of Nationality.
Bung will be there in force, for Bung is nothing if not
a politician ; but if the case of nationality against the
drink curse be forcibly put, let Bung the nationalist get
up and defend his position—if he can. Some resolu-
tions, even in Ireland, have a certain value, and we
would like to see a few resolutions dealing with the
Bung evil proposed at the next National Political Con-
vention. A resolution calling on the Convention as an
Irish national body to urge all public houses to be
closed on St. Patrick’s Day; another asserting that
£14,000,000 a year is an impossible drink burden for
Ireland to bear; another that no branch of the
United Irish League should have its habitation on a
licensed premises; another calling for a law to shut all
public houses at 9 o’clock, or earlier, on Saturday
nights, and shut them altogether on Sundays—these
| are a few resolutions that we hope will be proposed at
the next forthcoming Convention. The passing of these
resolutions—or their rejection—would at least be of
| some significance. The political party is there, and it
| is for the country to mould it. We do not want Mr.
O’Brien’s paper with its dozen Bung and its foreign
boot advertisements to split it up. = The nationalist
temperance men would de well to carry their campaign
vigorously into the next Convention. We wish not
the death of Bung but rather the life of the country, a
greater thing than Bung, big man though he is.

e IC‘HE lines of that great Catholic poet, Alexander
Pope, rush to one’s mind in surveying some events

of the week : —

“ As Hell’s dark gates the recreant I detest

Whose tongue belies the language of his breast.”
T refer to the action of certain gentlemen, his profes-
sional colleagues in the Catholic University School of

of D.Sc. Honoris Cause, on Professor Anthony Roche.
It is fairly well known who Dr. Roche is. The Leaner
has already shown that being an Trish Irelander he was
blackballed for the Fellowship of the College of Physi-
that being a Catholic he avoided Trinity
Collego; that as one of our first scientists he
gelected to teach at Maynooth College ; that as the best
pular lecturer of the day he was appointed to lecture

The next event was a case in Limerick where three

The last event goes to prove what contemptible crea-

train, and made not the slightest apology until the
Prince of the Church petrified him with a stare, Then,

THE ANTI-ENLISTING CRUSADE.

T is not without trepidation that I touch this ques-

[ tion of enlistment at all, knowing so well as I do
that in Ireland a man is more likely to be judged by his
imagined motives than by his actual arguments. Per-
haps I may ward off a few blows by saying that I don’t
want anybody to enlist; and that I am simply trying
to discuss the matter from the standpoint of economic
common-sense.
‘Why does Thomas Atkins himself enlist? Invariably
from one or other of two motives. An intent to pre-
serve and extend the British Empire, never, in the
least, forms either of these motives. The two motives
in question are: lack of employment, and temperamen-
tal inclination. It may be supposed that in a great in-
.dustrial country like England there is no end of open-
ings for a man willing to work with his hands; yet tho
proportion of persons relieved from the rates there is
greater than in Ircland. Consequently economic con-
“siderations play into the hands of the recruiting ser-
geant, and do much of ‘his work for him, or make the
.doing. of it easy for himself. But every unemployed
man does not enlist, else there would not be so many
.able-bodied persons in workhouses, prisons and: 'such
institutions, to say nothing of the number who live by
. loafing on their relations. Only some, not all, of the
Atkinses enlist through lack of employment. It is, T
‘think, highly probable that temperamental considera-

IDOLATOR.

 tions account for the greatest number of enlistments.

“Men who can’t “settle down '’ to anything else, some-
. how manage to reconcile themselves to settling down as
‘soldiers. It is strange, but nevertheless psychologically

“true, that a man who caunot govern himself, or use his

THE LEADER. 7 151

own time or powers advantageously, is often excelllent

.when under the control of a discipline administered by

someone whom he has to recognise as superior in power
to himsclf. Some, of course, enlist because they like
tho life, and would mot be happy at -any other. Men
have been known to commit suicide at the end of a long
service, through sheer unwillingness to relinquish the ~
calling they spent their lives at, and to part from their
comrades. Again, men who had finished their time
with the colours, have frequently re-enlisted in another
regiment under an assumed name, all through unwil-
lingness to give up the soldier’s life. Kipling’s ballad,
“Back to the Army Again,”’ is simply a humorous cele-
bration of this trait. Of all motives that lead men to
enlist, I should say that anything remotely resembling
patriotic intent forms the least part, if it ever forms
any. Neither Tommy, nor Sandy, nor Taffy ever en-
lists, I should say, under the influence of any Empire-
building intentions. English soldiers may be patriotic
cnough, in their own way, no doubt, but anything like
consclous patriotism is not the motive that leads them
to the depot.

And what leads the Irishman to enlist as a soldier in
the British army? Exactly the same motives as lead
Tommy, or Sandy to enlist, neither more nor less.
Some worthy people seem to think that every Irishman
ought to have an instinctive horror of soiling his palm
with the Saxon shilling, but, alas! Paddy has no such
horror at all. Patriotism no more keeps him out of the
army than patriotism drives the Britisher into it. The
idea that every Irishman ought to revolt against the
bare idea of joining the British army rests upon a very
notable illusion—the illusion that every Irishman is a
patriot almost by instinct. Certainly an illusion T call
it, for experience tells me that it is anything but true.
The average Irishman is not on fire with Irish patrio-
tism, and I venture to say that the whole Nationalist
movement is carried on by a strenuous minority of the
presumedly Nationalist people of this country, whom
the apathetic or negligent majority generally rally to
(in a fashion) in times of crisis and unusual excitement.
The queer, yet not unnatural, thing is, that when the
strenuous ones (for propagandist reasons, perhaps),
strive to make excitement the normal condition, the
apathetic crowd wax more apathetic than ever! There
are hundreds of thousands of Anglo-Celtic and Celtic
people in this country upon whom politics and public
affairs in general have no hold whatever. There is a
vast amount of apathy, inertia, and make-believe in
this country, backed with plenty of genuine indifference
and cynicism. People will tell you, “ Ah, I’ve seen so
many movements started!””  And of course that is
quite true; we have started a good many movements,
certainly, and the non-success of, perhaps, one or two of
them, now and then, has helped to *‘discourage the
others.”” Hearts will get sick of hope deferred, occa-
sionally, and proceed to take refuge in the philosophy of
Diogenes-—a Greeck sage who was more than merely
sceptical about the existence of honest men. “ Splits,”
too, are discouragers; they tend to fortify Diogenes in
his views. Besides, they are something that everyone
can “look on’’ at—and we Irish like looking on. Men
of some education, of high minds and forward views,
may possibly lift their heads above all this demoralising
atmosphere ; the majority, I fear, breathe this atmos-
phere as their native air—the Irish Tommy Atkins
among the rest. And then people turn round and won-
der how any Ivishman can join the British army! A
whole concourse of causes have operated towards vitiat-

R. VICTORY, in a recent art,icle in the LEADER 
. 
on the Irish scorn of English literature, does 
not, it seems to me, take _quite the proper view of tho 
phenomenon to which ho calls attention. Numbers of 
earnest and intelligent men and wumen, anxious for 
the triumph of Irish Ireland have, he tells us, devoted 
themselves exclusively to working on our Irish records 
and presenting in English . the result 0f their labour~ 
to their countrymen. 
Mr. Victory finds fault with 
them for their too exclusive attention to their subject, 
and suggests that if their labours do not result in 
literature it is because they do not read Shakespeare, 
Mazzini, Emerson, Carlyle and others, whom he calls 
master minds. 
Now, first of all, I do not consider 
1\iazzini or Rousseau to be in any true sense master 
minds in -tlie world of literature. 
I have known many 
who feel themselves on such writings as Mr. Victory 
suggests and failed to discover the glow of any great 
light in their eyes. 
The principal things in Emerson's 
writings are Pantheism in doctrine, and confusi0n in 
style. 
_He is said to have written each sentence 
separately, put them all in his hat, and then, as at an 
All Prize Drawing, to have shut his eyes and taken 
-them out one by one. 
Nor do I believe in Carlyle as 
a model of literal'.y form. 
Carlyle preached the gospel 
of silen·ce in about forty volumes. 
His silence was 
nearly as stentorious as that of Mr. \Villiam O'Brien 
during his last " final retirement from public life." 
And with regard to style Mr. O'Brien is a far better 
model than Carlyle. 
Carlyle never denounced the 
devil with half the dash, gusto or grace Mr. O'Brien 
displays when he begins to spill ink on the "nefarious 
Triumverate." 
There· is more literary form in one of 
the " Suppressed Speeches " than there is in all Carlyle 
ever wrote. If Mr. Victory is so anxious about literary 
form, would it not be well if he ordered a few thousand 
of these " Suppressed Speeches " so lately erupted on 
an astonished country. 
They could be got at a cheap 
rate-I got some of them for nothing. 
Ho could dis-
tribute them then, with the warning that these 
- . ·speeches were classics, but no~ guides. 
. 
But the choice of models 1s comparatively a small 
thing. 
A more important point is· the censure passed 
on a body of workers on our !rish records and legends. 
If they give themselves exclusively to the -:vor~ they are 
to be praised· and 1;1ot _bl~mod fo!· so ~ed1catmg them-
selves. 
They are JUst1fymg their existence, and. we 
have no right to ask them to flower out all at once mto. 
highly finished works of creative art. 
~he:y are sl~ow-
ing us that we have a past; they are brrngmg up 1!1to 
the national consciousness the thoughts, memones, 
stories, hopes, feelings and pictures of the past. 
The 
legends of the past had practically ceased to colour the 
national imagination when this movement began; now 
a knowledge of these is slowly spreading, and many are 
noticing with surprise that the Irish legends of old are 
curiously exemplified by modern· happenings in Ireland 
to-clay. 
In both there is a 11roneness to extremes, a, 
certain dreaminess, and frequently a want of propor-
tion 1!-nd a failure to get a real grip on the stern 
realities.of things. 
They may be, as Mr. Victory says, 
formless, but there is in them the beauty of vagueness, 
the large dim waving shapes, and much of tho gracious-
ness and charm which the Irishman at his best has 
always exercised over those who came within his-
s11her0 of influence. 
They may not be what a person 
wit;h Anglicised taste would call literature; they may 
not haye that souse of balance, that sense of proportion 
which colder blooded races, working more by the light 
of mere intellect could show in their legends; they may 
not have what Sir Horace Plunkett would call the 
masculine qualities, the inflexible will, the lust for 
gold, murder and revenge, the dreadful strength, the 
terrible tenacity of hate such as are displayed in the 
Nibelungenlied and in others of the saga of Northern 
Europe; they may not have these qualities, but they 
have this in them-they are Irish, they are ours, the 
call of blood is in them . The moulded thoughts, the 
embodied dreams, the environment of the past are· 
there. 
By such things a race lives. 
A race may sur-
vive in a starveliug way, feel on alien thoughts and 
memories, but it can only live when its records, legendsr 
songs and history impregnate the national conscious-
ness. 
For such an end the same scorners of English-
literature are working, and I hold it would be better 
let them be. 
The fine flower of literature will come in 
its time. Our past must first be recovered and made-
,t national possession. 
The Irishman who studies the history of his country,. 
and who sees the national character being gradually 
sapped, undermined and washed out by English in-
fluence, will hear with pleasure of this exclusive de-
votion to so needed a work. He will hear with a cer-
tain savage satisfaction that even a small body of his. 
countrymen have pronounced a comprehensive curse on. 
all English literature. It may be going too far, but 
your true Irishman will have a sympathetic under-
standing of such an attitude. It gives expression to 
t,he irreconcilable antagonism that exists between Irish 
ideals and English ideals. 
Formerly when we were 
utterly Irish, or mere Irish, as the English put it, we 
absorbed the foreigner who dwelt amongst us. 
Now 
we are dominated as a people by thoughts and tfiings 
English. 
Why even the -accent of the red-coated 
guttersnipe of English slumdom seems to exercise a fas-
cination over the very servant girls in this unfortunate 
land. 
The Irish scorn of English literature, I look 
upon as a healthy sign; it is the swing of the pendu-
lum, something too far, but it is prophetic of life for 
Irish Ireland. 
It is an ungracious thing to disagree ,vith one who 
is so anxious for the triumph of Irish Ireland as Mr. 
Victory, and it is a real pleasure to find myself in 
thorough accord with another portion of his pro-
gramme. 
He calls for a body of '' ardent literary and 
philosophical students who ·will acquaint themselves 
with the best and most finished products of the worlq's 
literature," and w,ill receiv.e into· minds thus cultured, 
the unminted gold of our records, pass them through the 
alembic of then· genius and give· to the world works of 
unrivalled excellence. It is a· large order,' but until 
some such centre of higher learning and higher train-
ing is formed in Ireland, we shall luwe to wait for our 
classics. 
Thero is no centre of higher learning in this 
country in harmony with the bent, the genius, the 
aspirations ancl ideals of our people, and the con-
sequence is that we have 110 Irish masterpiece in our 
day: there is no one to give the highest literary expres-
sion to the records of the past, or to the storm, t.he 
strife and pathetic struggles of recent times. 
Few 
seem to be conscious how much we lose, and stand to 
lose, by this barrenness. 
For one thing we are de-
prived of a great force, formation of life and char-
acter-a noble food for heart and soul, a food that 
would feed the Il'ish youth ancl malce the Irishman. 
Few seem to be conscious of this want, and fewer still 
inclined to examine into the cause of it. We have no 
National 'Uuiversit,y.

““ Nothing material appears against him, he having
become a convert to our religion.”’
—Lord Licutenant to Sovereign of Belfast, 1679.

N 1604, as we have seen, Chichester obtained a
grant of the lands in the neighbourhood of Belfast
Castle and commenced the building of the town. Bel-
fast is, therefore, almost exactly 300 years old. It was
the child as it is now the capital and pride of the
Plantation. I have briefly reviewed the political his-
tory of that Plantation; the political history of the
town is much the same. To, however, help the casual
reader to form an idea of the sort of population it now
contains I will give a few facts and figures relative to
same.

As I said (Chap. I.) Chichester’s earlier importations
were Englishmen, and the town remained principally
an English town for some time. Its trade, however,
being mostly with Scotland, and the Planters pouring
into the neighbouring counties being Scotch, Belfast be-
came by degrees Scotch too. The Irish naturally did
not get much of a look in during these early years. It
was not the place for them, judging by the poetic
address of the townspeople to William of Orange on
his arrival to fight James: —

““ Ah! mighty Prince, how do our sorrows swell,
What tongues or pens can our great miseries tell,
Who in the midst of Satan’s subjects dwell.”

Accordingly we find that in 1708 the Sovereign of the
town could discover only seven Papists amongst those
under his jurisdiction, and, as late as the United Irish
era, they were so few that even the McCracken family
were dubious as to whether their brother, Henry Joy,
was wise in agitating' for the emancipation of a class
about whom he knew so little.  The Dissenters were
then the most prominent sect of the population. The
jnjustices, on religious and other grounds, which they
had been suffering since the Revolution have been re-
ferred to. It was these injustices that roused their
tempers against England during the period 1778—’98.
With the passing of the Act of Union, as we have seen,
they became loyalists, and later on, when Gladstone
came over to the HHome Rule idea, they became Liberal
Unionists. In common with the Episcopalians and
the Catholics they have enormously increased in num-
bers during the 19th century, and are still the most
numerous sect in the city. ;

Now for a few figures. In 1802 the population of
Belfast was, roughly, 19,000; in 1831 it was 52,287 ;
in 1871 it was 174,412; and in 1901 it was 34&?,1804
These figures give an idea of the wonderful rapidity of
the growth of the town during the 19th century.
Within that period it renewed itself over and over
again, so to say. One would think that in the midst
of such bustle of progress there would' be no time nor
inclination to think of such a thing as bigotry.
Wouldn’t there, though? The new blood in the popu-
lation was naturally mostly drawn from the circum-
jacent counties, especially from Down and Antrim, and
in these two counties, at the time Belfast was com-

the increase came from across the Channel, but they
didn’t liberalize the atmosphere much. ‘Whatever

admit that the man who has been so often called a

gentle and each dreary scene, and catching, from the
loveliness as well as the dreariness of the ocean and

good of man in his overwhelming admiration of the
mighty works of God.”

It may be interesting to note a few points common
to these distinguished Catholics.  All were men of
strong character, except perhaps Hofer.  All their
marriages seem to have been extremely happy. Five,
out of eight, were barristers, but four gave up the
profession and the remaining one was no longer prac-
